title: Nodal superconductivity with spin-triplet component in a noncentrosymmetric weakly-correlated metal
authors: Marcel Strohmeier, Andriy Smolyanyuk, Karsten Held, Michael Smidman, Geetha Balakrishnan, Wolfgang Belzig, Elke Scheer, Angelo Di Bernardo
abstract: In conventional superconductors, Cooper pairs form in an even-parity spin-singlet state. Noncentrosymmetric superconductors, which lack inversion symmetry, exhibit antisymmetric spin-orbit coupling (ASOC) that can combine even-parity spin-singlet and odd-parity spin-triplet pairs into a mixed-parity order parameter. Spin-triplet components are highly beneficial for superspintronic devices. Whether ASOC alone $-$ without strong electronic correlations $-$ is sufficient to generate a measurable triplet component remains a central open question. Here, we resolve this question in Nb$_{18}$Re$_{82}$ (Nb-Re), a weakly-correlated noncentrosymmetric metal whose superconducting pairing symmetry has been actively debated. Using low-temperature scanning tunneling spectroscopy on single crystals with four distinct crystallographic orientations, find a pronounced orientation-dependent anisotropy in the local density of states. Supported by a symmetry-constrained model, we show that the complete set of tunneling spectra requires a mixed-parity order parameter with the triplet amplitude reaching up to half of the singlet component. These results reconcile the conflicting reports in the literature on Nb-Re and demonstrate that ASOC is sufficient to foster a sizable spin-triplet component even without strong electronic correlations, suggesting that mixed-parity superconducting states may be more widespread than previously assumed. Since Nb-Re can be readily fabricated in thin-film form, these findings position it as an accessible platform for superspintronic devices and establish orientation-resolved tunneling spectroscopy as a general protocol for the detection of mixed-parity order parameters.
summary: This work investigates the superconducting pairing symmetry in the noncentrosymmetric metal $	ext{Nb-Re}$ using orientation-dependent scanning tunneling spectroscopy. By analyzing the local density of states across multiple crystal faces, the authors demonstrate that the superconducting order parameter must be a mixture of spin-singlet and spin-triplet components. This resolves a long-standing debate by showing that ASOC alone is sufficient to induce significant triplet pairing, positioning $	ext{Nb-Re}$ as a platform for superconducting spintronics.
main_problem: The central question addressed is whether antisymmetric spin-orbit coupling (ASOC) alone, without invoking strong electronic correlations, is sufficient to generate a measurable spin-triplet component in the superconducting order parameter of a noncentrosymmetric superconductor (NCS).
main_result: The study demonstrates that ASOC is sufficient to foster a sizable spin-triplet component in $	ext{Nb-Re}$, evidenced by orientation-dependent superconducting gap structures, reconciling conflicting literature reports.
method: The research combines low-temperature scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS) on single crystals with symmetry-constrained theoretical modeling of the superconducting order parameter.
model_or_system: The system is $	ext{Nb}_{18}	ext{Re}_{82}$, a weakly-correlated noncentrosymmetric metal. The model incorporates a mixed-parity order parameter combining even-parity spin-singlet and odd-parity spin-triplet components.
key_observables: Orientation-dependent local density of states (DoS) measured via $dI/dV$, and the zero-bias conductance (ZBC) dependence on magnetic field.
important_parameters: The ratio of triplet to singlet amplitude ($\Delta_o/\Delta_e$), and the superconducting gap energy ($\Delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed spectral features can be accurately modeled by a mixed-parity order parameter, and that the measured anisotropy is intrinsic to the pairing symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures show STM $dI/dV$ spectra on different crystal faces ((001), (212), (110)) exhibiting varying gap structures, and plots illustrating the required combination of singlet and triplet components to fit the full set of experimental data.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical framework for mixed-parity superconductivity in NCS, presents STS measurements across multiple crystal orientations, and uses a symmetry-constrained model to fit the observed anisotropic DoS, concluding that ASOC is the primary driver for triplet pairing.
why_interesting: The finding that ASOC alone drives mixed-parity superconductivity provides a clean, correlation-free mechanism for unconventional pairing, which is highly relevant for understanding pairing mechanisms in complex materials relevant to quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electronic structure and crystallographic characterization of Nb-Re. (A) Electronic band structure based on DFT calculations (B) Corresponding three-dimensional FS constructed from the band structure, illustrating the complex multi- band topology. The color scale reflects the magnitude of the Fermi velocity vF. (C) FS decomposed into eight individual band contributions. (D) Cubic α-Mn unit cell of Nb-Re with the colored crystallographic planes indicating the investigated surface orientations. (E) Single-crystal X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern measured on the (113) surface, confirming the crystallographic quality and orientation of the sample.

FIG. 2. Tunneling spectroscopy on Nb-Re thin films. (A) Scheme of the low-temperature STM experiment and the normal metal/superconductor tunneling configuration. (B) 3D representation of an atomic force microscope image showing the surface topography of the Nb-Re thin film. (C), (D) BCS-like tunneling spectra measured at various temperatures T and magnetic fields B⊥. The extracted superconducting gap energy and Dynes parameter Γ are shown on the right. (E) Selection of non-BCS-type dI/dV spectra measured across the Nb-Re surface (F) V-shaped tunneling spectrum with its evolution under an increasing B⊥. (G) Zero-bias conductance (ZBC) as a function of the external magnetic field. (H)...

FIG. 3. Characterization of Nb-Re single crystals with (001) and (113) surface orientations. (A) Photographs of the two Nb-Re single crystals, along with a sketch of the sample geometry illustrating the investigated crystallographic surface orientations. (B) Electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization showing the Kikuchi patterns (left) and the corresponding pole figures (right) for the two colored surface orientations in (A). (C) Representative BCS-type tunneling spectra acquired on the (001) and (113) surfaces.

FIG. 4. Tunneling spectroscopy on a Nb-Re single crystal with (212) surface orientation. (A) Representative dI/dV tunnel spectra measured on the (212) surface of a Nb-Re single crystal. (B) Field-dependent evolution of a double-peak spectrum under a magnetic field B⊥. (C) Anisotropic suppression of the sc gap on the (212) surface, with dI/dV measured consecutively in a 2 T magnetic field applied perpendicular and parallel to the sample surface, respectively. (D) Enlarged superconducting gap observed on the (110) surface orientation. (E) Summary of the even- and odd-parity irreducible gap basis functions compatible with the point group of Nb-Re. FIG. 5. Analysis of the enlarged superconducting gap acquired on the (110) surface orientation. (A) Two- dimensional k-space cut through the even-parity dz2-orbital-type order parameter (Γ3) along the (110) plane. Adding an odd-parity component (Γ3) significantly alters the lobe structure. Particularly, the variable scale ratio A = ∆L1/∆L2 of the orthogonally oriented lobes manifests as energy-dependent in-gap feature in the DoS. (B) Calculated DoS with variable contributions of the odd-parity component.
